segments.s042-g9: "از آنکو" is an archaic compound meaning "from that which / since that which" and introduces the clause; it is followed by "ز هر گونه دیده جهان", where the poetic preposition "ز" (=از) means "from/through" and "هر گونه دیده جهان" literally "every kind of the world's eye(s)" = "every sort of beholder". The whole segment sets up the source/cause: "from that which has been seen by every eye in the world."
segments.s042-g10: "شده آشکارا" is a perfect participle plus adverb (literally "has become evident/openly"), describing the prior "that which"; the command "برو بر نهان" uses the imperative "برو" (go/turn) plus the preposition "بر" in the sense "toward/to" and the adjective/noun "نهان" (the hidden). Together this gives the instruction: "[that which] has been made manifest — go/turn to the hidden."
segments.s042-g11: "گراینده" is an agent participle from a verb meaning "to turn/guide/direct," here functioning as a noun: "one who directs/wields." It is followed by the coordinated objects "تیغ و گرز گران" — "تیغ" (sword) and "گرز گران" (mace + adjective "گران" = "heavy"), with the adjective after the noun as is normal in Persian poetry.
segments.s042-g12: "فروزنده" is another agent/epithet meaning "illuminating/brightening" used as a substantive here; "نامدار" is an epithet "renowned/illustrious" modifying it, and "افسران" (helmets) is the object/thing brightened. The phrase reads as a tersely ordered epithet: "the renowned brightener of helmets."
segments.s042-g13: "نماینده" is a noun meaning "representative/embodiment." It is followed by a string of modifiers: "شب" (night) and "به‌روز سپید" — "به‌روز" here means "of the day/by day" and "سپید" "bright/white." In this compact poetic order the phrase names someone as the embodiment of night and of the bright daytime.
segments.s042-g14: "گشاینده" is an agent noun "opener/revealer," taking the following noun "گنج" (treasure) as its object; "پیش امید" places that action "before/in the presence of hope" (پیش = "before, in front of"). The whole unit means "the revealer/opener of treasure before hope."
segments.s042-g15: "همه رنجها" = "all the sufferings/hardships." "گشته" is the perfect participle from "شدن" used as "have become/turned," and "آسان" is the predicate adjective "easy." The final word "بدوی" is an archaic adverbial (literally "original/primitive") here modifying the clause and can be read as "as of old / in the primary way," so the segment reads "all hardships have become easy (as before)."
segments.s042-g16: The imperative "برو" (go) issues the command; the rest is the goal/result: "روشنی اندر آورده روی" — literally "bring/brought light into/on the face." "اندر آورده" is a perfect participle used with the imperative senseually as "bring," and "روی" = "face" is the locus. So together: "Go, bring light upon the face."
segments.s042-g33: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. از بودنش بگذرد is a verbal clause literally 'when of his being passes' — بگذرد is present/subjunctive/present-future form of گذشتن used here to mean 'have passed' (classical usage). روز چند follows with روز 'day' and چند meaning 'a few/several', so the whole segment means 'when a few days had passed of his presence'.
segments.s042-g34: فرستید is the past (3rd person plural) of فرستادن 'to send' ('they sent'). با introduces accompaniment 'with'. زی here functions as a genitive/qualifying element attached to منش (rendered 'of/with ... character' in classical phrasing). منش ارجمند = 'an esteemed/noble character or bearing' (ارجمند = esteemed). The phrase together means 'they sent (him) with an esteemed/noble bearing.'
segments.s042-g35: نهادند is the past (3rd person plural) of نهادن 'to place/appoint' (they placed/they appointed). بر marks the beneficiary/target 'on/for'. نامه‌بر is 'letter‑carrier / courier' (the appointed person). مهر شاه here is 'the king's seal' (مهر = seal), i.e. they placed the king's seal on the courier.
segments.s042-g36: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. ایوان 'iwan/porch' is the point of departure. بر here means 'toward/to' and ایرج is the personal name Iraj (destination). گزین کرد is a two-part verbal phrase 'گزین' (choose) + auxiliary/verb کرد, forming the past 'chose'. راه 'road/way' is the object (placed at the end for poetic order): 'he chose the road from the iwan toward Iraj.'
segments.s042-g37: بشد (بـ + شد) is an archaic/classical perfective/past-use of شدن meaning 'he set out / it came to be' (here 'he set out' or 'it happened that he set out'). با introduces accompaniment 'with'. تنی چند is a fixed expression 'a few persons / a small company' (تنی = 'a body/one, some' + چند 'several'). برنا و پیر are attributes of that company: برنا 'young' and پیر 'old' — 'young and old'.
segments.s042-g38: چنان چون is a correlative pair 'in such a way that / because' (here introducing the reason/condition). بود is the past tense of بودن 'was'. راه را uses را as the definite/accusative marker attached to 'راه' ('the road'); in poetry this marked noun functions as the logical subject complement. ناگزیر is an adjective 'inevitable / compelled / unavoidable'. The clause means 'for the road was unavoidable' or 'since there was no avoiding the road.'
segments.s042-g17: نخواهم is the 1st-person present negative of خواستن (’to want’): 'I do not want / I will not'. همی is the old/poetic continuous particle (here emphasizing an ongoing wish or habit) and follows the verb; خویشتن را places 'myself' as a marked (definite) object with را. کلاه is the direct object 'cap / hat' (here a personal ornament/insignia). Word order is verb + continuous particle + object (marked by را) + noun.
segments.s042-g18: This is a negated list of things the speaker refuses: آگنده (filled, laden) modifies گنج (treasure/hoard). The coordinating و links the items: 'neither ... nor ... nor ...'. تاج 'crown' is a parallel item; گاه here closes the list as 'place/seat/throne' in context (poetic use). The particle-and-conjunction sequence simply enumerates what the speaker will not have.
segments.s042-g19: سه فرزند را places 'three children' as the definite/affected object (را) before the verb, following Persian SOV patterns for marked objects. خواهم is 'I want/wish' (present), and آرام و ناز are complements describing the desired state ('calm/at ease' and 'pampered/caressed').
segments.s042-g20: از آن پس که is a temporal/subordinating phrase 'from that after/now that' introducing the reason or time clause. که introduces the subordinate clause; دیدیم is the 1st-person plural past 'we saw' or 'we experienced'; رنج دراز is the object phrase 'long suffering'. Together: 'since/after we have seen endured long suffering.'
segments.s042-g21: برادر is the noun 'brother'; کزو is an older/poetic compound meaning 'from whom / because of whom' (ک + زو/زو = از او). بود is the past copula 'was', and دلتان ('your heart' with the 2nd-person plural possessive -تان) + به درد is the idiom 'in pain / grieved'. The clause literally says 'He was a brother from whom/for whom your hearts were in pain.'
segments.s042-g22: وگر چند is a concessive/contrastive opening: وگر (archaic 'if / but if / yet') plus چند ('though / however' here used adverbially). هرگز normally means 'ever / never' (context determines polarity); نزد means 'near / in the presence of'; باد is the literal 'wind' (here 'cold wind' = hardship or a cold/blighting force); سرد is the adjective 'cold'. The clause is elliptical (no finite verb) and reads as a concessive fragment: 'and yet, however, never near the cold wind.'
segments.s042-g23: دوان آمد is a light verbal phrase meaning 'came running / rushed in' (دوان as adverb/participle + آمد 'came'). از بهر is a fixed older prepositional phrase 'for the sake of / on account of'; آزارتان is 'your trouble/affliction' (آزار with the 2nd-person plural possessive -تان). The whole: 'He came running for your sake / because of your distress.'
segments.s042-g24: که introduces a subordinate explanatory clause 'for / because'; بود is the past copula 'was'; آرزومند is an adjective/participle 'desirous/longing'; دیدارتان is a noun meaning 'your meeting / the seeing of you' with the 2nd-person plural possessive. Together: 'for he was desirous (longing) of meeting you.'
segments.s042-g25: بیفگند is a poetic/past 3rd-singular form (from افکندن) meaning ‘cast/placed’ or here ‘bestowed’; the verb precedes its object in the line. شاهی شما را places ‘kingship’ as the direct object (را marks definiteness/object), and گزید is a second past verb ‘he chose/selected’—the two verbs form a compact, parallel statement: he conferred kingship and chose (you).
segments.s042-g26: چنان ‘so/thus’ begins a result clause introduced by کز (a relative/comparative particle here ‘that/so that’). ره نامداران is a genitival phrase ‘the path of the renowned’, with نامداران functioning as a plural substantive/adjective ‘the famous/illustrious ones’. سزید is a stative/past-form verb ‘befit/deserve’ placed at the end, meaning ‘was fitting/you deserved (it)’. Overall: ‘so that you are fit for the path of the famous.’
segments.s042-g27: ز تخت اندر آمد is a verbless/compound movement phrase used idiomatically: ز تخت (from the throne) + اندر آمد (came down/entered) = ‘he came down from the throne’. Then به زین برنشست is a short clause ‘onto the saddle he sat’ (به = to/onto, زین = saddle, برنشست = sat upon). The verbs are simple past forms placed after their adverbials.
segments.s042-g28: برفت is an archaic/poetic past of رفتن meaning ‘he went away/rode off’ (here followed by و linking actions). میان بندگی را is the object phrase ‘the bonds/ties of servitude between (them)’ with را marking it as definite/object. ببست (past) is ‘he tied/bound’. The sequence lists successive actions: he departed and then bound the ties of service.
segments.s042-g29: بدان کو is a dative/relative phrase ‘to that one who…’ (بدان = to that; کو = who). به سال از شما کهترست spells out the comparison ‘he is a year (به سال) younger than you’ (کهترست = ‘is lesser/younger’). Word order is typical older Persian: the comparative phrase follows the relative marker and the copular/predicative adjective comes at the end.
segments.s042-g30: نوازیدن is an infinitive ‘to flatter/indulge/caress’ used as a noun (the act of flattering). کهتر here denotes ‘the younger/inferior one’ and stands as the object of نوازیدن; اندر is the locative ‘in/among’. خورست is an adjectival/predicative form that here means ‘is fitting/acceptable’ (poetic), so the clause states that flattering the younger is proper. The main verb copula is implicit, as is common in poetry.
segments.s042-g31: گرامیش دارید is an exhortative present/imperative-like formula ‘hold him dear / esteem him’ (گرام(ی) = dear, دارید = you have/you hold). و links the clauses. نوشه خورید puts the object نوشه ‘a draught/sip’ before the verb خورید; here خورید is a hortatory/imparative-like form ‘take/drink’. Both verbs address the addressee in a polite/poetic imperative manner.
segments.s042-g32: چو = ‘when’; چو ... شد introduces a temporal subordinate clause ‘when … has become’. پرورده شد تن = ‘the body has been reared/raised’ (پرورده = nurtured, شد = became/was). روان پرورید is the main (hortatory) verb: روان ‘the soul/spirit’ as object, پرورید ‘nurture’ (imperative/command form). The verb-final order is normal; the line says: when the body is reared, nurture the soul.
segments.s042-g1: This clause means 'a certain letter (was) written by the king of the land.' یکی functions as an indefinite 'a certain' before نامه (the direct object). بنوشت is a past-tense verb 'wrote' (classical form with a prefixed b-), placed before the subject شاه زمین for poetic inversion; شاه زمین = 'king of the land/world' (subject).
segments.s042-g2: This is a double dative introduced by the preposition به: 'to the Khavar‑khodā (lord of the East) and to the sālar of China.' خاور and خدای form a compound/title ('east' + 'lord/God' used as a title) without an explicit ezāfe, and سالار چین = 'the commander/lord of China.' و links the two recipients.
segments.s042-g3: سر نامه کرد literally 'he made the head (opening) of the letter' — کرد is the past tense 'did/made' used to mean 'began' or 'headed' the letter. آفرین functions as 'Creator' (present‑stem meaning 'he who creates') and خدای is vocative 'O God' here; together they form the opening address of the letter.
segments.s042-g4: This clause is an optative/wish formulated in classical style. کجا هست = 'where (you) are' (هست = copula/exist, with the pronoun omitted), و links the next clause; باشد is subjunctive/optative 'may (you) be/remain' and همیشه به جای = 'always in (your) place/station.' The pronoun 'you' is understood and omitted as usual.
segments.s042-g5: چنین گفت = 'thus he said' (classical preface to reported speech). کاین is the fused conjunction 'که + این' meaning 'that this', introducing the content of his remark. نامه پندمند = 'a letter full of counsel/admonition' (پندمند = admonitory/filled with advice) — adjective follows the noun in this phrase.
segments.s042-g6: به نزد = 'in the presence of' or 'before'; دو خورشید is a poetic epithet 'the two suns' (metaphor for two great princes). گشته is a perfect/participial sense 'has become/been made/brought' and بلند 'lofty' describes their exalted status — together indicating the letter 'had been elevated/presented' before these exalted figures.
segments.s042-g7: This string uses repeated دو ('two') as a poetic intensifier before a series of epithets: سنگی ('stone‑like, stern'), جنگی ('warlike, martial'), and then شاه زمین = 'kings of the land/earth.' The adjectives qualify the kings; word order reflects classical poetic compression and apposition.
segments.s042-g8: میان = 'among' or 'in the midst of'; کیان refers to the Kianian kings/dynasty. چون = 'like/as', and درخشان نگین = 'a shining gem' (درخشان adjectival, نگین = gem). The clause says these two kings stand among the Kians like a brilliant gem.

sentences.s042-s5: From what has been made manifest to every kind of eye in the world, turn toward the hidden.
sentences.s042-s6: One who directs the sword and the heavy mace, the renowned brightener of helmets.
sentences.s042-s7: An emblem of night and of the bright day; the opener of treasure in the presence of hope.
sentences.s042-s8: All hardships have become easy (as of old); go and bring light onto the face.
sentences.s042-s17: When a few days had passed, they sent him forth with an esteemed, noble bearing.
sentences.s042-s18: They set the king's seal upon the courier; from the iwan he chose the road toward Iraj.
sentences.s042-s19: He set out with a small band, young and old, for the road was unavoidable.
sentences.s042-s9: I do not wish for myself any cap—neither a hoard filled with treasure, nor a crown, nor a throne.
sentences.s042-s10: I want my three children to be at peace and pampered, now that we have endured long suffering.
sentences.s042-s11: There was a brother because of whom your hearts were pained; and yet, however, never (was he) near the cold wind.
sentences.s042-s12: He ran (came running) for your sake to relieve your trouble, for he was eager to see you.
sentences.s042-s13: He bestowed kingship upon you and chose you so that you might be worthy of the path of the renowned.
sentences.s042-s14: He rose from the throne, mounted the saddle, rode away, and bound the bonds of servitude between (them).
sentences.s042-s15: To him who is a year younger than you, flattering (the) younger one is fitting.
sentences.s042-s16: Hold him dear and take the draught; when the body is reared, nurture the soul.
sentences.s042-s1: The king of the land wrote a certain letter to the lord of the East and to the commander of China.
sentences.s042-s2: He headed the letter: 'O Creator‑God — wherever you are, may you remain forever in your place.'
sentences.s042-s3: Thus he said that this admonitory letter had been brought before the two exalted suns.
sentences.s042-s4: Two stern, two warlike, two kings of the earth — among the Kians like a shining gem.
